news.title: Jimmy Fallon swipes at Biden’s upcoming memoir, jokes Kamala Harris wrote the end of it
news.text: Late-night host Jimmy Fallon swiped at former President Joe Biden on Wednesday during "The Tonight Show" after the ex-president announced he would be releasing a memoir in November. Biden's memoir, "Promise Me, America," will chronicle his presidency and reveal the "deeply agonizing calculation" behind his decision to withdraw from the 2024 presidential race, according to a press release announcing the book. The memoir is set to come out on Nov. 17, about two weeks after the midterms. Fallon showed a picture of the cover of the book and said, "The book was written by a ghost writer, Joe Biden." EX-BIDEN STAFFER BLAMES BIDEN'S 'HUBRIS' FOR THE 'HELLSCAPE THAT WE LIVE IN': REPORT "It’s the second book Biden has written," the late-night comedian said. "The first was ‘The Odyssey.’" He quipped that the book was about what the former president remembered from his presidency and said, "So it's three pages." Fallon then brought former Vice President Kamala Harris into his bit and added, "It’s kind of a strange book because toward the end it starts being written by Kamala Harris." WATCH: KAMALA HARRIS TORCHED OVER ‘ZERO SENSE’ WNBA PEP TALK IN LATEST ‘WORD SALAD’ MOMENT Fox News Digital reached out to representatives of the former president for comment about the jokes but didn't immediately hear back. Biden announced the book in a video posted to X Wednesday. He added that he has been receiving treatment for his cancer diagnosis and that it has been going "really well." "It's about the challenges we face as a nation and about the decisions I made and why I made them, leading the country through COVID, rebuilding our economy and restoring our democracy after the attack on January 6th, ending our nation's longest war in Afghanistan, strengthening NATO and supporting Ukraine," Biden said. Lindy Li, a former top Democratic fundraiser, said Biden was only doing himself a disservice by promising the public another explanation of why he decided to run for re-election in 2024 despite mounting concerns about his age and cognitive performance. CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE In her view, Biden’s upcoming memoir will only serve to reopen painful wounds that Democrats have struggled to move past since the 2024 election loss. In a way, she believes the book is a bit of a microcosm of Biden’s presidency. CLICK HERE TO DOWNLOAD THE FOX NEWS APP "They’re thinking, ‘Why can’t you just shut the f--- up?’ Prior to me leaving the party, they wanted nothing to do with this guy. It was so bad that when, finally, Biden exited on July 21, there was no goodbye party other than a staffer one in the Rose Garden. There was nothing," Li said. Fox News' Ashley J. DiMella contributed to this report.
news.summary: Jimmy Fallon roasted Joe Biden's upcoming memoir with jokes about it being written by a "ghostwriter," Biden, and quipping Kamala Harris wrote the end.

news.title: A Look Inside Motown’s Famous Hitsville U.S.A.
news.text:
Motown Records is one of the most iconic record labels in music history. Founded by Berry Gordy in 1959, the label became one of the country’s most successful Black-owned businesses. Icons such as Marvin Gaye, Diana Ross, the Temptations, Stevie Wonder and the Jackson 5, just to name a few, made countless hits at the label’s first headquarters, Hitsville U.S.A. Now, Motown’s original HQ is being renovated thanks to a $1 million donation from The Links Inc., a social organization of over than 15,000 prominent Black women leaders, including Kamala Harris and Condoleezza Rice. This major gift helps support the museum’s decade-long expansion project.

As we wait with bated breath for the expanded museum to open in the summer of 2027, we’re taking a look inside Hitsville U.S.A. and the fascinating exhibitions the museum already has to offer.

Berry Gordy Boulevard

Before you even reach Hitsville U.S.A., you will know you’re nearby by seeing the Berry Gordy Jr. Boulevard street sign. According to the Detroit real estate firm the Perna Team, the street was given the secondary name in 2007 to honor the legendary Motown founder, who gave so many Black icons the recording space they deserved.

The Outside of the Museum

The museum itself is hard to miss. Painted with blue doors and a huge Hitsville U.S.A. sign on the front, it’s an exciting welcome to museum visitors, who can immediately imagine icons like Marvin Gaye walking through its doors to make music magic.

Related: Do You Know the Sad Story of Amy Winehouse? The White English Soul Singer Who Black People Loved

Studio A Control Room

Once you get inside Studio A, you’ll see everything left as it was originally. From worn-out wooden floors where musicians were no doubt tapping their feet to hit records to a control room that has a schedule of artist recording sessions lined up on its chalkboard.

Grand Piano In Studio A

The grand piano in Studio A first got to Motown in 1967 and was used by artists such as Stevie Wonder. It was restored in 2012 by iconic Beatles member Paul McCartney, who donated funds to repair it after a 2011 visit to the museum. Now it stands as it once did in the 1960s, ready to play some catchy tunes.

Gold Records

Walking around Hitsville U.S.A. means you’ll also get to see the first gold plaque the recording studio ever earned, which was Smokey Robinson and the Miracles’ 1961 track “Shop Around.”

Staff Time Clock

You can imagine the daily life of staff at Hitsville U.S.A. by taking a look at their punch cards kept right beside the studio’s original time clock.

Sheet Music for “My Girl”

As you walk through the museum, you’ll see that many aspects of the studio have been maintained in their original state. The sheet music for the Temptations’ hit song “My Girl” remains on one of the music stands, just as it was when it was recorded.

Secretary’s Desk

The secretary at Hitsville U.S.A. had one of the busiest jobs in the studio, with three phones and a typewriter on her desk – the first stop for hundreds of iconic musicians who walked through the door.

Microphone Wires in Studio A

The microphone wires used by artists to record their songs still hang in Studio A today. Although they’re no longer in use, we can imagine this equipment being used to record songs that remain on folks’ playlists today.

The Entrance to Berry Gordy’s Apartment

The first floor of Hitsville U.S.A. was the recording studio, but once you head upstairs, you enter Berry Gordy’s former apartment. Just like the downstairs of the museum, Gordy’s apartment is maintained in its original state.

Candy Machine

If staff and musicians needed a little bit of energy to keep them going, there was a vending machine stocked with candy inside the studio.

Living Area

Gordy’s apartment isn’t grand, but it was still a nice space with a television set in one corner and a comfy floral-patterned chair.

More of Berry Gordy’s Apartment

Further into Berry Gordy’s apartment is an orange leather couch, which sits in front of a playpen for his children.

Packaged Vinyls and Old Newspapers

In the dining area, instead of plates and cookware, you’ll find vinyl records being packaged and old editions of the Billboard newspaper, no doubt to check what was at the top of the charts.

The Other Departments for Hitsville

As Hitsville U.S.A. became more successful, Gordy couldn’t keep his whole operation in a two-story apartment anymore. He bought multiple houses on the same street to serve as different departments, such as publishing, finance, and administration.

The Former Finishing School for Artists

Another house on the block was designated as Motown’s finishing school. It was created to teach artists how to dance and conduct themselves in public. This was a unique aspect of Motown, as other record studios were not doing the same, giving the artists who attended the school that extra professional edge, according to the performing arts center, the Smith Center.
news.summary:
Hitsville U.S.A. is expanding with more exciting exhibitions in 2027. As we wait to see what's new, we're taking a look at the place where many Motown hits were made.
